title: Deep Vein Thrombosis
objectives: Apply Wells criteria for DVT probability assessment

vignette.text:
A 58-year-old woman presents with 3 days of left leg swelling and pain. She had knee replacement surgery 3 weeks ago. She has been less active since surgery.

Past Medical History: Osteoarthritis, Hypertension
Medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily, was on enoxaparin post-op but stopped 1 week ago
Social History: Non-smoker, no recent travel

physicalExam.extremities: Left leg: 3 cm larger circumference than right at calf, warmth, mild erythema, tenderness along deep veins, no cords palpable. Right leg normal.

questions.question: Calculate this patient's Wells score for DVT:
questions.explanation: Wells criteria: Recent surgery +1, Localized tenderness +1, Entire leg swollen +1, Calf >3cm difference +1, Pitting edema +1 = 5 points (high probability). Alternative diagnosis less likely adds +1 if applicable.

questions.question: With high Wells score, what is the next step?
questions.explanation: High probability Wells → go directly to compression ultrasound. D-dimer is only useful to rule out DVT in LOW probability patients. High probability needs imaging regardless of D-dimer.

questions.question: What is appropriate initial anticoagulation for acute proximal DVT?
questions.explanation: DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) are first-line for most patients with acute DVT. Can be started without bridging (unlike warfarin). Exceptions: cancer (LMWH preferred), renal failure, pregnancy.

questions.question: Standard duration of anticoagulation for provoked DVT (surgery)?
questions.explanation: Provoked DVT (major transient risk factor like surgery) = 3 months. Unprovoked DVT = consider extended therapy. Cancer-associated = treat while cancer active. Recurrent unprovoked = usually lifelong.

keyTakeaways: D-dimer useful only in LOW probability patients
DOACs first-line for most acute DVT
Duration: provoked = 3 months; unprovoked = consider extended
IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected deep vein thrombosis?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in deep vein thrombosis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of deep vein thrombosis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing deep vein thrombosis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A 35-year-old presents with petechiae, confusion, renal insufficiency, fever, and platelets of 18,000 with schistocytes on smear. What is the most critical immediate intervention?

options.text: Emergent plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) — do not wait for ADAMTS13 results
options.explanation: This pentad (thrombocytopenia, microangiopathic hemolytic anemia with schistocytes, neurologic symptoms, renal dysfunction, fever) is classic TTP. Per ASH guidelines, plasma exchange must be initiated IMMEDIATELY — mortality without treatment exceeds 90%. Do NOT wait for ADAMTS13 activity levels; start treatment on clinical suspicion.

options.text: Platelet transfusion to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
options.explanation: Platelet transfusion in TTP is CONTRAINDICATED (relative) as it can fuel thrombotic microangiopathy and worsen organ damage. Platelets are consumed in microvascular thrombi, not bleeding.

TTP is distinguished from DIC by NORMAL coagulation studies (PT, PTT, fibrinogen). DIC shows prolonged PT/PTT and low fibrinogen. Treatment differs fundamentally — TTP requires plasmapheresis, not anticoagulation.

options.text: Obtain ADAMTS13 activity level and treat based on results
options.explanation: ADAMTS13 testing takes 2-3 days. With suspected TTP, mortality approaches 90% without treatment. Plasma exchange must begin on clinical suspicion while awaiting confirmatory testing.
